schedule.talkTitle: Investment Practices of Leading Charitable Foundations
schedule.notes: Week 8 Day 1 of Talk 6 (Week 1 of 1, Single-Week Talk). Read & Overview focus: 3% cost problem, drift toward complexity, why foundations destroy charitable capital through avoidable costs. Core frameworks: 3% Cost Problem (arithmetic of costs destroying wealth in low-return environments), Three-Layer Consultant Incentives (perverse structures adding cost and misalignment), Concentration vs. Diversification Trade-off (rational concentration when you understand the business). 5 actionable takeaways: calculate total cost drag, audit for layered complexity, question professional complexity, apply cost-return arithmetic test, recognize overconfidence trap. 5 case snapshots: LTCM collapse (lollapalooza effect of overconfidence + sophistication + leverage), GM fourth door mistake (professionalism over observation), Woodruff Foundation Coca-Cola concentration (wisdom of concentration through understanding), Berkshire low-cost approach (simplicity beats complexity), Fund of Funds revival (social proof recreating failed past models). Mental model drill on Inversion with Day 1 prompts: biggest insight challenging current thinking (professionalism doesn't equal better results), assumption Munger would question (diversification and professional management being inherently good). 3 reflection questions: audit of total investment costs, workplace professionalism complexity analysis, latticework building for incentive-caused bias/overconfidence/social proof/inversion. Personal commitment: calculate total annual investment costs as percentage of principal. Talk 6 focuses on foundation investment practices, showing how incentive-caused bias, overconfidence, and social proof lead foundations to adopt costly, complex consultant structures that destroy charitable capital. Next: Week 8 Day 2 - Mental Models (Incentive-Caused Bias, Overconfidence, Social Proof, Man-with-a-Hammer). Message delivered successfully.

schedule.focus: Incentive-Caused Bias, Man-with-a-Hammer, Overconfidence, Social Proof, Lollapalooza
schedule.notes: Week 8 Day 2 of Talk 6 (Mental Models Focus). Four mental models: Incentive-Caused Bias (three-layer consultant structure creating perverse incentives, GM's fourth door mistake), Man-with-a-Hammer Syndrome (foundations copying Yale's complex fund-of-funds, LTCM collapse as lollapalooza effect), Overconfidence Bias (90% of Swedish drivers rate above average, investment professionals overestimate abilities, Feynman's self-criticism principle), Social Proof (Yale model contagion through institutions, Demosthenes quote 'What a man wishes, he will believe', consultant feedback loop). Lollapalooza Effect defined: multiple biases reinforcing each other (incentives + overconfidence + social proof + availability + liking + authority). Arithmetic: 17% - 3% costs - 5% donations = 9% net growth (good times); 5% - 3% costs - 5% donations = -3% shrinkage (bad times). Foundations lose 3% annually in bad markets. Berkshire's approach: <0.1% costs, zero turnover, few positions, deep understanding. Key takeaways: structure incentives first, question social proof, recognize overconfidence, watch for lollapalooza, simplicity beats complexity. Reflection questions: if consultant wanted max impact/min cost, what would they recommend? What professional confidence might be unwarranted? What are you doing 'because everyone else does it'? Are multiple forces pushing you toward complexity? Next: Week 8 Day 3 - Inversion. Message delivered successfully.

schedule.talkTitle: Inversion: How to Destroy Foundation Wealth
schedule.focus: Inversion Thinking, How to Destroy, Feynman's Self-Criticism Principle, Arithmetic of Disaster
schedule.notes: Week 8 Day 3 of Talk 6 (Inversion Focus). Inversion thinking: how to guarantee failure, then avoid that path. How to destroy foundation wealth: 5% returns - 3% costs - 5% donations = -3% annual shrinkage. 26% destroyed over 10 years, 45% destroyed over 20 years. How to create professional disaster: overconfidence + sophistication + leverage + no self-criticism (GM fourth door, LTCM collapse). How to waste charitable capital: layered consultants, high turnover, blind copying, no self-criticism. The croupier's take: investment costs destroying wealth. Feynman's self-criticism principle: 'You must not fool yourself—and you're the easiest person to fool.' How to avoid fooling yourself: seek disconfirming evidence, challenge assumptions, have trusted advisors challenge your thinking. Solution: eliminate complex consultant layers (1 layer instead of 3), reduce turnover to near zero, choose simplicity (indexing OR concentration, not middle ground), accept realistic returns (5%, not 17%), maintain self-criticism (Feynman principle), align incentives (single layer). Solution arithmetic: reduce costs from 3% to 1% or <0.1% (Berkshire). Bad markets: 5% - 1% - 5% = -1% shrinkage (vs -3%), Good markets: 17% - 1% - 5% = 11% net growth (vs 9%). Berkshire's costs <0.1%: 5% - 0.1% - 5% = -0.1% shrinkage, 17% - 0.1% - 5% = 11.9% net growth. Simplicity as virtue: complexity is expensive (3% cost drag), simplicity is effective (<0.1% costs, more capital retained), middle ground is worst (high costs, poor returns). Key takeaways: use inversion (ask how to guarantee failure, then avoid it), apply Feynman's self-criticism (seek disconfirming evidence), embrace simplicity (avoid complexity), align incentives (single layer). Reflection questions: if foundation returns 5%, how much grows after costs? What professional decisions have overconfidence + no self-criticism? What assumptions might be wrong? How would you guarantee failure in your work? If you save 2% costs annually, how much good over 20 years? Next: Week 8 Day 4 - Career Applications. Message delivered successfully.

Week 10 Day 1 of Talk 8 (Week 1 of 1, Single-Week Talk). Read & Overview focus: Munger's prophetic warning about accounting practices that would trigger Enron/WorldCom era scandals. Core frameworks: Stock Option Accounting Anomaly (GAAP loophole where options are tax-deductible but not expensed on income statement), 'Bit by Bit' Fraud Technique (gradual distortions accumulate, avoiding detection), Earnings Smoothing Incentive (analysts reward smooth growth, creating perverse incentives). 5 actionable takeaways: read stock options footnote first, question 'too smooth' earnings, avoid buyback + option combos, check auditor tenure, calculate true economic earnings. 5 case snapshots: Quant Tech fictional case (founder vs. successor eras), Enron (Munger's prediction confirmed), WorldCom (capitalizing expenses), Berkshire (anti-Quant Tech), contemporary tech startups (Quant Tech reborn). Mental model drill on Incentive-Caused Bias with Day 1 prompts: biggest insight challenging current thinking (smooth earnings != good management), assumption Munger would question (trust in audited statements). 3 reflection questions: audit of stock options footnote reading, workplace incentive alignment analysis, latticework building for incentives across domains. Personal commitment: read stock-based compensation footnote of one company. Talk 8 focuses on accounting fraud and stock option manipulation, showing how incentives, flawed accounting rules, and human psychology combine to create predictable financial disasters. Next: Week 10 Day 2 - Mental Models (Incentive-Caused Bias, Inversion, Inconsistency-Avoidance, Man-with-a-Hammer). Message delivered successfully.

schedule.focus: Better Approaches to Economic Thinking (Multidisciplinary Economics)
schedule.notes: Week 12 Day 1 of Talk 9 (Week 2 of 2, Multi-Week Talk). Better Approaches focus: Multidisciplinary economics, scenario thinking vs forecasting, circle of competence applied to economics. Core frameworks: Multidisciplinary Economics (combine insights from psychology, biology, history, practical business experience), Scenario Thinking vs Forecasting (build systems robust across scenarios instead of predicting), Circle of Competence Applied to Economics (recognize limits of knowledge and complexity). 5 actionable takeaways: add psychological layers to every economic analysis, replace forecasts with scenario analysis, practice physics envy detection, build economic latticework, prioritize rough-but-right over precise-but-wrong. 5 case snapshots: Berkshire vs EMH (beating the market for decades), LTCM collapse (Nobel laureates failed due to physics envy), 2008 Financial Crisis (models ignored incentives and psychology), Market Bubbles Throughout History (psychology trumps models), Inversion Case (forecasting trap - confidence kills). Mental model drill on Multidisciplinary Economics with Day 1 prompts: biggest insight challenging current thinking (elegance doesn't equal accuracy), assumption Munger would question (reliance on economic models/forecasts). 3 reflection questions: audit of economic analysis for flawed assumptions, workplace incentives for false confidence in predictions, latticework building across disciplines. Personal commitment: audit one economic analysis/forecast by adding psychological factors and questioning assumptions. Talk 9 Week 2 focuses on constructive solutions to economic thinking problems: incorporate psychology, accept uncertainty, use multidisciplinary mental models. Next: Week 12 Day 2 - Mental Models for Economic Analysis. Message delivered successfully.

masterResearchSchedule.notes: Completed for Week 10 (Talk 8 starts May 24, 2026). Note: Full transcript not accessible; research based on detailed summaries and excerpts from multiple sources.

Completed for Week 11 (Talk 9 starts May 31, 2026). Key insights: Physics envy, micro vs. macro economics split, rationality assumptions are wrong, markets often inefficient. Major case studies: LTCM (Nobel laureates failed), 2008 crisis (academic models missed it), Berkshire vs. EMH (beating the market). Core message: Economics is valuable as a framework but flawed as a predictor. Needs psychology, accepts uncertainty, abandons physics envy.
